    "model": "gpt-5.3-codex",
    "personality": "pragmatic",
    "collaboration_mode": {
      "mode": "plan",
      "settings": {
        "model": "gpt-5.3-codex",
        "reasoning_effort": "medium",
        "developer_instructions": "# Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository is the working MetroSense codebase: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer (✅ **exists and wired**, current status: skeleton with google_search tool only)\n- Treat the current implementation as the baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n- **Status**: ~40% complete. Auth flow, backend-to-agent routing, and database schema are functional. Data seeding, custom agent tools, risk cards/artifacts, streaming, and message persistence are planned.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Chat UI, streaming presentation, structured response rendering, browser-facing state management.\n- `server/`\n  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\n- `agents/`\n  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\n- `server/app/api/`\n  - FastAPI routes and request/response boundaries.\n- `server/app/services/`\n  - Backend business logic and internal orchestration code.\n- `server/app/db/`\n  - Database models, metadata, and session management.\n- `server/tests/unit/`\n  - Fast isolated tests for backend logic and simple route behavior.\n- `server/tests/integration/`\n  - App-level and database-backed tests.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - The agents service runs behind a lightweight proxy that enforces the `X-Internal-Token` shared secret. The backend calls the proxy on port 8020, and the proxy forwards to the ADK server on port 8021. `/health` stays public for simple availability checks.\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8021 .`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token && uv run uvicorn app.proxy:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n\n### Implemented (✅)\n- **Backend:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout/me with JWT + httponly cookies), health checks, database schema (9+ models for weather/AQI/flooding/outages/traffic), `/api/chat` endpoint\n- **Backend-to-Agent:** Proxy service with `X-Internal-Token` validation, session routing to ADK\n- **Frontend:** Chat UI (MessageBubble, MessageList, InputBar), auth context (login/signup/logout), RiskCard and ArtifactRenderer components, health polling\n- **Tests:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout), password hashing, health checks, basic chat endpoint\n\n### Planned (📋)\n- **Agent Tools:** Custom tools for flood-risk assessment, AQI lookup, outage status, traffic conditions, document retrieval\n- **Data Seeding:** Load CSV data (DataSet_MetroSense/) and documents (Documents_Metrosense/) into database\n- **Structured Responses:** Risk cards and artifacts emitted by agent layer; backend extracts and shapes for frontend\n- **Streaming:** Server-sent events (SSE) or WebSocket for streamed text responses\n- **Message Persistence:** Store chat history in database; load conversation context for agents\n- **Chat Response Tests:** Extend coverage for agent output parsing, risk card generation, artifact rendering\n\n### Current Test Coverage\n- Backend: Health, auth (signup/login/logout/me), password hashing, basic chat routing\n- Frontend: Component renders (no E2E tests written yet)\n- Agents: smoke_test.py validates proxy routing only (not domain logic)\n\n### Next Steps (Recommended Order)\n1. Implement data loader to seed MetroSense CSVs and documents\n2. Add custom agent tools (flood-risk, aqi-lookup, outage-status, traffic)\n3. Implement structured response generation (risk cards/artifacts from agent)\n4. Add message history persistence and context retrieval\n5. Implement streaming responses (SSE or WebSocket)\n6. Extend test coverage for full end-to-end flows\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- **Important:** The CSV data files (DataSet_MetroSense/) and documents (Documents_Metrosense/) exist in the repo but are **not yet loaded** into the database. Implement a data loader before building agent tools that depend on MetroSense domain knowledge.\n- The agent service skeleton runs and routes to google_search tool, but custom domain tools (flood-risk, aqi, outage, traffic) do not yet exist. Add tools incrementally as matching backend data becomes available.\n- If current behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}

## Entry 22
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T09:25:37.412Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T09:25:37.412Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_h1l7YvMWYybX49nabFBSWm7C",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 0bd2a6\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1901\nOutput:\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:63:**Decision:** JSON output is enforced as a **typed contract at every agent boundary** using Pydantic v2 strict mode. Every domain agent returns a Level-1 typed payload. The Orchestrator runs a pure Python merge function to produce the Level-2 Unified Risk Scorecard. No LLM call is involved in this merge step — it is deterministic.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:74:**Three output modes exist:**\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:80:If a chart would genuinely improve understanding such as trend queries or comparative queries, the system appends a follow-up prompt: \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" User replies yes and the chart is generated. This keeps the user in control and avoids over-engineering the response layer.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:84:- **Button trigger** — dedicated UI button asks user for a ward/location and generates full scorecard for that zone\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:85:- **NL trigger** — if the user's query contains words like \"risk\", \"scorecard\", \"vulnerability\", \"assessment\", \"how vulnerable is\" for a specific place, scorecard generation kicks in automatically\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:101:The system is conversational and agentic. No server-sent events, no streaming token output. Responses are returned as complete payloads from agent execution.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:119:Every agent response includes a `data_freshness` block telling the user when the underlying data was last updated. A BBMP disaster officer acting on 6-hour-old rainfall data needs to know that. This is non-negotiable for government internal use.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:151:Chat Agent (main conversational interface — only agent user talks to)\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:163:**Root Agent:** Pure routing and state management. Never talks to the user directly. Owns parallel dispatch, global guardrails, and the Level-1 to Level-2 scorecard merge.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:165:**Chat Agent:** The only agent that has a conversation with the user. Owns session memory, NL synthesis, citation formatting, chart follow-up prompts, and scorecard triggers. It calls domain agents directly — no round-trip back through the Root Agent.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:167:**Domain Agents:** Pure domain reasoning. They are sub-agents registered under the Chat Agent. They own their own Postgres tools and Agentic RAG document tools directly. They never receive user input directly.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:169:**Important guardrail implication:** Because the Chat Agent is the actual entry point for user input, input filtering, injection detection, domain relevance checks, and citation grounding must all live at the Chat Agent level — not assumed to be handled upstream.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:209:│  • Only agent the user interacts with                                │\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:238:        │ POST /chat { session_id, message, user_role }\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:245:        │ POST /agent/run { session_id, message, history[], user_role }\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:249:  • Determines output mode\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:285:**Long query handling:** Complex compound queries can take 5-10 seconds. Since there is no SSE the Frontend shows a loading state. This is acceptable for internal government use. Users are not consumer users expecting instant responses.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:492:After any trend or comparative answer, Chat Agent evaluates whether a chart would improve understanding. If yes it appends \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" On user confirmation a chart spec is generated and returned. Charts are never generated automatically — always requires explicit user confirmation.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:906:user_role              -- bbmp_internal / planner / logistics\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:917:role                   -- user / assistant\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:971:| historical_city_event | Derived from documents — lives in RAG pipeline output |\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1109:Raw PDFs are kept for audit — a user can always ask to see the original source document. Indexes and section text files are what the agents actually use at query time.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1185:How JSON index content and section text are formatted and injected into\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1199:- Tool-level protected data access control by user role — a public session simply does not have protected tier tools registered\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1221:| Chat Agent | Hallucination filter | NL output checked against source payloads |\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1261:Expected output assertions:\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1276:Option A — Citation coverage (always on, built into guardrails): Every claim in the NL output must have a citation referencing a DB record ID or document section ID. Claims without citations are flagged automatically.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1278:Option B — LLM-as-judge (eval suite only, selective): Applied to the two highest-consequence output types:\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1334:│  • Chart rendering when user confirms follow-up chart prompt         │\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1375:  \"user_role\": \"bbmp_internal\"\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1397:  \"user_role\": \"bbmp_internal\"\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1510:│   │   ├── confidence_gate.py           # Block outputs below threshold\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1638:- How Postgres rows are formatted before injection — JSON, table, or natural language\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1639:- How document section text is formatted and truncated before injection\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1656:- Postgres unreachable — what does the user see?\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1659:- All agents return low confidence — how is this communicated to the user?\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:61:    │   ├── chat_agent.py             # chat_agent — sole user-facing agent\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:86:All routes prefixed `/internal`. Protected by a FastAPI dependency that reads `X-Internal-Token` from the request header and compares to `settings.agent_internal_token`. Returns HTTP 401 on mismatch. This dependency is **not** the same as `require_user` — no DB lookup, no JWT.\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:162:    user_role: Mapped[str]\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:175:    role: Mapped[str]                # \"user\" | \"assistant\"\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:209:    user_role: str = \"user\",\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:219:    user_message: str,\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:225:- Inserts TWO rows: one `role = \"user\"`, one `role = \"assistant\"` (same turn)\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:292:The only agent that synthesises a natural language response to the user.\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:548:ChatResponse returned to user\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:551:The DB write happens **after** the ADK call succeeds. If the DB write fails, the response is still returned to the user (log the error, don't fail the request). This is a best-effort persistence approach — data availability > perfect persistence.\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:686:| `resolve_location` returning zero results | Chat agent instruction handles `LOCATION_NOT_FOUND` case and asks user to rephrase |\ndocs/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:688:| DB write fails after ADK response | Best-effort persistence — log error, return response to user regardless |\n"
  }
}

{
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_h1l7YvMWYybX49nabFBSWm7C",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 0bd2a6\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1901\nOutput:\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:63:**Decision:** JSON output is enforced as a **typed contract at every agent boundary** using Pydantic v2 strict mode. Every domain agent returns a Level-1 typed payload. The Orchestrator runs a pure Python merge function to produce the Level-2 Unified Risk Scorecard. No LLM call is involved in this merge step — it is deterministic.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:74:**Three output modes exist:**\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:80:If a chart would genuinely improve understanding such as trend queries or comparative queries, the system appends a follow-up prompt: \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" User replies yes and the chart is generated. This keeps the user in control and avoids over-engineering the response layer.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:84:- **Button trigger** — dedicated UI button asks user for a ward/location and generates full scorecard for that zone\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:85:- **NL trigger** — if the user's query contains words like \"risk\", \"scorecard\", \"vulnerability\", \"assessment\", \"how vulnerable is\" for a specific place, scorecard generation kicks in automatically\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:101:The system is conversational and agentic. No server-sent events, no streaming token output. Responses are returned as complete payloads from agent execution.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:119:Every agent response includes a `data_freshness` block telling the user when the underlying data was last updated. A BBMP disaster officer acting on 6-hour-old rainfall data needs to know that. This is non-negotiable for government internal use.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:151:Chat Agent (main conversational interface — only agent user talks to)\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:163:**Root Agent:** Pure routing and state management. Never talks to the user directly. Owns parallel dispatch, global guardrails, and the Level-1 to Level-2 scorecard merge.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:165:**Chat Agent:** The only agent that has a conversation with the user. Owns session memory, NL synthesis, citation formatting, chart follow-up prompts, and scorecard triggers. It calls domain agents directly — no round-trip back through the Root Agent.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:167:**Domain Agents:** Pure domain reasoning. They are sub-agents registered under the Chat Agent. They own their own Postgres tools and Agentic RAG document tools directly. They never receive user input directly.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:169:**Important guardrail implication:** Because the Chat Agent is the actual entry point for user input, input filtering, injection detection, domain relevance checks, and citation grounding must all live at the Chat Agent level — not assumed to be handled upstream.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:209:│  • Only agent the user interacts with                                │\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:238:        │ POST /chat { session_id, message, user_role }\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:245:        │ POST /agent/run { session_id, message, history[], user_role }\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:249:  • Determines output mode\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:285:**Long query handling:** Complex compound queries can take 5-10 seconds. Since there is no SSE the Frontend shows a loading state. This is acceptable for internal government use. Users are not consumer users expecting instant responses.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:492:After any trend or comparative answer, Chat Agent evaluates whether a chart would improve understanding. If yes it appends \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" On user confirmation a chart spec is generated and returned. Charts are never generated automatically — always requires explicit user confirmation.\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:906:user_role              -- bbmp_internal / planner / logistics\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:917:role                   -- user / assistant\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:971:| historical_city_event | Derived from documents — lives in RAG pipeline output |\nUrbanClimate_AI_Architecture.md:1109:Raw PDFs are kept for audit — a user can always ask to see the original source document. ## Entry 29
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T09:25:44.846Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T09:25:44.846Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_5gSPD24CU1VIuH8FA3vu4uKu",
    "output": "Chunk ID: eb473d\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1175\nOutput:\n\n---\n\n## 2. Architecture Decisions & Rationale\n\n### Decision 1 — Agent Count: 1 Orchestrator + 5 Domain Agents, No Sub-Agents\n\n**What was considered:** Original design had 1 Orchestrator + 5 Domain Agents + 2-3 Sub-Agents per domain = 12-15 total agents.\n\n**Problem:** Too many agents managing context and state creates debugging nightmares. A failure anywhere in the chain is hard to trace. Testing compound queries requires mocking 4-5 agent boundaries simultaneously. The Orchestrator ends up owning complex glue logic which contradicts the thin orchestrator principle.\n\n**Decision:** Flatten to **1 Orchestrator + 5 Domain Agents**. Each domain agent directly owns its tools — both Postgres query tools and Agentic RAG document tools. Sub-agents are a refactoring step for later if a specific domain agent becomes too large, not a starting point.\n\n**Why this works for Bangalore scope:** This is a city-specific internal system in testing. Query volume and complexity do not justify the overhead of sub-agent management.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 2 — Structured JSON Output: A Discipline, Not an Agent\n\n**What was considered:** A dedicated JSON Output Agent that formats final responses.\n\n**Problem:** Adds a redundant LLM call, introduces a fragile parsing step, and creates an unnecessary agent boundary.\n\n**Decision:** JSON output is enforced as a **typed contract at every agent boundary** using Pydantic v2 strict mode. Every domain agent returns a Level-1 typed payload. The Orchestrator runs a pure Python merge function to produce the Level-2 Unified Risk Scorecard. No LLM call is involved in this merge step — it is deterministic.\n\n```\nDomain Agent Output  →  Pydantic Level-1 Payload  (per domain)\nOrchestrator Merge   →  Pydantic Level-2 Scorecard (unified)\n```\n\n---\n\n### Decision 3 — Output Modes: Text First, Progressive Enhancement\n\n**Three output modes exist:**\n\n**Mode 1 — Conversational Text (default)**\nEvery query gets a clean NL answer. This is always the primary response.\n\n**Mode 2 — Chart (on follow-up)**\nIf a chart would genuinely improve understanding such as trend queries or comparative queries, the system appends a follow-up prompt: \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" User replies yes and the chart is generated. This keeps the user in control and avoids over-engineering the response layer.\n\n**Mode 3 — Risk Scorecard (triggered explicitly)**\nTwo trigger mechanisms:\n- **Button trigger** — dedicated UI button asks user for a ward/location and generates full scorecard for that zone\n- **NL trigger** — if the user's query contains words like \"risk\", \"scorecard\", \"vulnerability\", \"assessment\", \"how vulnerable is\" for a specific place, scorecard generation kicks in automatically\n\n**Important:** \"summary\" is intentionally excluded from NL triggers because it is too generic and can be used in non-scorecard contexts.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 4 — Zone Registry: Bangalore Major Wards Only\n\nThe system recognizes Bangalore's major BBMP wards as canonical zones. A `location_master` table with canonical names, aliases, and geometry is the grounding layer. This prevents the LLM from misinterpreting freeform location references.\n\nExamples: Bellandur, Sarjapur, Koramangala, Whitefield, Hebbal, Manyata Tech Park, Yelahanka, BTM Layout, Jayanagar, Peenya, KR Puram, Silk Board.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 5 — No SSE, Purely Agentic\n\nThe system is conversational and agentic. No server-sent events, no streaming token output. Responses are returned as complete payloads from agent execution.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 6 — Authentication: Deferred\n\nCurrently internal government use only. No authentication layer is implemented in v1. When the system is made public, role-based access will be added — specifically to protect the protected data tier covering power grid topology, sewage infrastructure, and building plans. This is a known open item.\n\n**Two data tiers defined for when auth is added:**\n- **Public tier** — weather, AQI, lake levels, traffic, flood history, ward-level risk scores\n- **Protected tier** — sewage and drainage infrastructure detail, power grid topology, specific building plans. BBMP and government roles only.\n\n**Security principle locked in:** Security cannot live only in the prompt. Protected tier tools must not be exposed to public role sessions regardless of prompt content. Tool-level access control, not prompt-level.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 7 — Data Freshness Indicator\n\nEvery agent response includes a `data_freshness` block telling the user when the underlying data was last updated. A BBMP disaster officer acting on 6-hour-old rainfall data needs to know that. This is non-negotiable for government internal use.\n\n"
  }
}
```

Architecture Decisions & Rationale\n\n### Decision 1 — Agent Count: 1 Orchestrator + 5 Domain Agents, No Sub-Agents\n\n**What was considered:** Original design had 1 Orchestrator + 5 Domain Agents + 2-3 Sub-Agents per domain = 12-15 total agents.\n\n**Problem:** Too many agents managing context and state creates debugging nightmares. A failure anywhere in the chain is hard to trace. Testing compound queries requires mocking 4-5 agent boundaries simultaneously. The Orchestrator ends up owning complex glue logic which contradicts the thin orchestrator principle.\n\n**Decision:** Flatten to **1 Orchestrator + 5 Domain Agents**. Each domain agent directly owns its tools — both Postgres query tools and Agentic RAG document tools. Sub-agents are a refactoring step for later if a specific domain agent becomes too large, not a starting point.\n\n**Why this works for Bangalore scope:** This is a city-specific internal system in testing. Query volume and complexity do not justify the overhead of sub-agent management.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 2 — Structured JSON Output: A Discipline, Not an Agent\n\n**What was considered:** A dedicated JSON Output Agent that formats final responses.\n\n**Problem:** Adds a redundant LLM call, introduces a fragile parsing step, and creates an unnecessary agent boundary.\n\n**Decision:** JSON output is enforced as a **typed contract at every agent boundary** using Pydantic v2 strict mode. Every domain agent returns a Level-1 typed payload. The Orchestrator runs a pure Python merge function to produce the Level-2 Unified Risk Scorecard. No LLM call is involved in this merge step — it is deterministic.\n\n```\nDomain Agent Output  →  Pydantic Level-1 Payload  (per domain)\nOrchestrator Merge   →  Pydantic Level-2 Scorecard (unified)\n```\n\n---\n\n### Decision 3 — Output Modes: Text First, Progressive Enhancement\n\n**Three output modes exist:**\n\n**Mode 1 — Conversational Text (default)**\nEvery query gets a clean NL answer. This is always the primary response.\n\n**Mode 2 — Chart (on follow-up)**\nIf a chart would genuinely improve understanding such as trend queries or comparative queries, the system appends a follow-up prompt: \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" User replies yes and the chart is generated. This keeps the user in control and avoids over-engineering the response layer.\n\n**Mode 3 — Risk Scorecard (triggered explicitly)**\nTwo trigger mechanisms:\n- **Button trigger** — dedicated UI button asks user for a ward/location and generates full scorecard for that zone\n- **NL trigger** — if the user's query contains words like \"risk\", \"scorecard\", \"vulnerability\", \"assessment\", \"how vulnerable is\" for a specific place, scorecard generation kicks in automatically\n\n**Important:** \"summary\" is intentionally excluded from NL triggers because it is too generic and can be used in non-scorecard contexts.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 4 — Zone Registry: Bangalore Major Wards Only\n\nThe system recognizes Bangalore's major BBMP wards as canonical zones. A `location_master` table with canonical names, aliases, and geometry is the grounding layer. This prevents the LLM from misinterpreting freeform location references.\n\nExamples: Bellandur, Sarjapur, Koramangala, Whitefield, Hebbal, Manyata Tech Park, Yelahanka, BTM Layout, Jayanagar, Peenya, KR Puram, Silk Board.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 5 — No SSE, Purely Agentic\n\nThe system is conversational and agentic. No server-sent events, no streaming token output. Responses are returned as complete payloads from agent execution.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 6 — Authentication: Deferred\n\nCurrently internal government use only. No authentication layer is implemented in v1. When the system is made public, role-based access will be added — specifically to protect the protected data tier covering power grid topology, sewage infrastructure, and building plans. This is a known open item.\n\n**Two data tiers defined for when auth is added:**\n- **Public tier** — weather, AQI, lake levels, traffic, flood history, ward-level risk scores\n- **Protected tier** — sewage and drainage infrastructure detail, power grid topology, specific building plans. BBMP and government roles only.\n\n**Security principle locked in:** Security cannot live only in the prompt. Protected tier tools must not be exposed to public role sessions regardless of prompt content. Tool-level access control, not prompt-level.\n\n---\n\n### Decision 7 — Data Freshness Indicator\n\nEvery agent response includes a `data_freshness` block telling the user when the underlying data was last updated. A BBMP disaster officer acting on 6-hour-old rainfall data needs to know that. This is non-negotiable for government internal use.\n\n"

}

**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T09:25:44.847Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_o85icqPsV6Q6tygbCpyxxhOI",
    "output": "Chunk ID: e14cc5\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 586\nOutput:\nair quality, power grid, and traffic. Please rephrase your question.\"\n\nLOCATION_NOT_FOUND = \"I couldn't find '{name}' in the Bengaluru location registry. \nPlease use a ward name or neighbourhood (e.g. 'Bellandur', 'Koramangala', 'Whitefield').\"\n\nNO_DATA_AVAILABLE = \"No recent data is available for {location} on {domain}. \nThe dataset covers historical records — please check the data freshness.\"\n```\n\n---\n\n## 8. Session Persistence Flow\n\n```\nUser → POST /api/chat\n    │\n    ▼\nchat route (FastAPI)\n    │ ─── calls ──→ agent_proxy.get_chat_response(settings, db_session, session_id, message)\n    │                   │\n    │                   ├─ 1. _ensure_session (ADK InMemory)\n    │                   ├─ 2. POST /run → ADK → root_agent → chat_agent → domain agents\n    │                   ├─ 3. extract message_text from events\n    │                   ├─ 4. conversation_service.upsert_session(db_session, session_id)\n    │                   ├─ 5. conversation_service.append_turn(db_session, session_id, ...)\n    │                   └─ 6. await db_session.commit()\n    │\n    ▼\nChatResponse returned to user\n```\n\nThe DB write happens **after** the ADK call succeeds. If the DB write fails, the response is still returned to the user (log the error, don't fail the request). This is a best-effort persistence approach — data availability > perfect persistence.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Environment Variables\n\n### Backend additions (`server/.env`)\n\nNo new variables needed — the backend already has `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN`. The internal routes use it for verification.\n\n### Agents additions (`agents/.env`)\n\n```\nBACKEND_INTERNAL_URL=http://localhost:8010    # or http://server:8010 in Docker\nDOCUMENTS_PATH=/documents                     # mounted volume path\nAGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token       # same as backend\nAGENT_MODEL=gemini-2.5-flash\nGOOGLE_API_KEY=<your_key>\n```\n\n### Docker Compose changes (agents service)\n\n```yaml\nagents:\n  ...\n  environment:\n    - BACKEND_INTERNAL_URL=http://server:8010\n    - DOCUMENTS_PATH=/documents\n    - AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=${AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN}\n    - AGENT_MODEL=${AGENT_MODEL:-gemini-2.5-flash}\n  volumes:\n    - ./server/Documents_Metrosense:/documents:ro\n```\n\n---\n\n## 10. Backend Config Update\n\nIn `server/app/core/config.py`:\n\n"
  }
}
```

Call `conversation_service.upsert_session(db_session, session_id)`\n2. Call `conversation_service.append_turn(db_session, session_id, message, message_text, agents_invoked=[], latency_ms=<elapsed>)`\n3. `await db_session.commit()`\n\nThe chat route in `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` already gets `db_session` injected via `Depends(db_session)` — pass it through to `get_chat_response`.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Agent Architecture\n\n### 5.1 Agent hierarchy (exact ADK structure)\n\n```\nroot_agent  (LlmAgent, name=\"root_agent\")\n    sub_agents:\n        └── chat_agent  (LlmAgent, name=\"chat_agent\")\n                sub_agents:\n                    ├── flood_vulnerability_agent\n                    ├── heat_health_agent\n                    ├── infrastructure_agent\n                    └── logistics_agent\n```\n\nADK `sub_agents` are registered as a list on the parent agent. The parent can transfer control to them via tool call or explicit hand-off. The root_agent routes to chat_agent for all domain traffic.\n\n### 5.2 `metrosense_agent/agent.py` — root_agent\n\n```python\nfrom google.adk.agents import Agent\nfrom metrosense_agent.subagents.chat_agent import chat_agent\nfrom metrosense_agent.prompts import ROOT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION\n\nroot_agent = Agent(\n    name=\"root_agent\",\n    model=os.getenv(\"AGENT_MODEL\", \"gemini-2.5-flash\"),\n    description=\"MetroSense root orchestrator — routes all queries to the chat agent.\",\n    instruction=ROOT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION,\n    sub_agents=[chat_agent],\n)\n```\n\nroot_agent instruction:\n- Never answer directly. Always delegate to chat_agent.\n- Domain: Bengaluru climate, flood, AQI, power, traffic only.\n- Reject non-Bengaluru geography queries before delegation.\n- Reject harmful / non-domain queries with a fixed refusal string.\n\n### 5.3 `subagents/chat_agent.py` — chat_agent\n\nThe only agent that synthesises a natural language response to the user.\n\nTools on chat_agent: `resolve_location`, `list_document_indexes`\n\nSub-agents: `flood_vulnerability_agent`, `heat_health_agent`, `infrastructure_agent`, `logistics_agent`\n\nInstruction responsibilities:\n1. **Input guardrail** — if the query is not about Bengaluru climate/infrastructure, respond with: `\"I can only answer questions about Bengaluru's climate, flood risk, air quality, power grid, and traffic. Please rephrase.\"`\n2. **Location resolution** — always call `resolve_location` before forwarding to domain agents. Canonical `location_id` must be established.\n3. **Routing** — map intents to agents:\n   - Flood / lake / inundation / drainage → `flood_vulnerability_agent`\n   - AQI / air quality / health / heat / UHI → `heat_health_agent`\n   - Power / outage / BESCOM / tree fall / wind → `infrastructure_agent`\n   - Traffic / corridor / ORR / delay / logistics → `logistics_agent`\n   - Multi-domain → delegate to multiple agents in sequence, then synthesise\n4. **Scorecard trigger** — if query contains `risk`, `scorecard`, `vulnerability`, `assessment`, `how vulnerable is` → call ALL four domain agents and synthesise the Unified Risk Scorecard format.\n5. **Citation rule** — every factual claim in the response must reference either a DB record timestamp or a document section ID. No unsourced numerical claims.\n6. **Data freshness** — include the observed_at timestamp of the most recent data point used in the response.\n\n### 5.4 `subagents/flood_agent.py` — flood_vulnerability_agent\n\nTools:\n- `get_weather_current(location_id)` — from `tools/shared/weather_tools.py`\n- `get_weather_historical(location_id, hours)` — from `tools/shared/weather_tools.py`\n- `get_lake_hydrology(lake_id, limit)` — from `tools/flood_tools.py`\n- `get_flood_incidents(location_id, limit)` — from `tools/flood_tools.py`\n- `list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)` — from `tools/shared/document_tools.py`\n- `fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)` — from `tools/shared/document_tools.py`\n\nInstruction:\n- Primary signal: `rainfall_intensity_mm_hr` from weather + `fill_percentage` + `overflow_status` from lake hydrology\n- Secondary signal: `flood_incident` history for the location (frequency, severity, water_depth_cm)\n- Document grounding: read flood study index → identify relevant sections (ones with `contains_thresholds: true` for the ward) → fetch section → extract threshold values → compare against live data\n- Barricade recommendation: only if `rainfall_intensity_mm_hr > threshold_from_document` AND (`fill_percentage > 85` OR `overflow_status = true`)\n- Output in structured format (see §7)\n- Known monitored lake IDs (from golden data): Bellandur, Varthur, KR Puram, Hebbal — map these from location resolution\n\n### 5.5 `subagents/heat_agent.py` — heat_health_agent\n\nTools:\n- `get_aqi_current(location_id, limit)` — from `tools/heat_tools.py`\n- `get_aqi_historical(location_id, days)` — from `tools/heat_tools.py`\n- `get_weather_current(location_id)` — shared weather tool (temperature, humidity)\n- `get_ward_profile(ward_id)` — from `tools/heat_tools.py`\n- `list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)` — document tools\n- `fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)` — document tools\n\nInstruction:\n- `aqi_value` + `aqi_category` + `dominant_pollutant` drive advisory specificity\n- `elderly_population_pct` + `children_population_pct` from ward_profile → sensitive population multiplier\n- `green_cover_pct` → UHI intensity factor\n- WHO thresholds applied per pollutant type (these are embedded in instruction, not from DB)\n- Requires specific advisory text per pollutant: PM2.5 → \"avoid outdoor exercise\", NO2 → \"keep windows closed\", etc.\n\n### 5.6 `subagents/infra_agent.py` — infrastructure_agent\n\nTools:\n- `get_weather_current(location_id)` — shared\n- `get_weather_historical(location_id, hours)` — shared (wind gust trend)\n- `get_power_outage_events(location_id, window_days)` — from `tools/infra_tools.py`\n- `list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)` — document tools\n- `fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)` — document tools\n\nInstruction:\n- Primary signal: `wind_gust_kmh` (NOT `wind_speed_kmh`) from most recent weather observation\n- Only `outage_type != 'planned'` events are used for historical calibration\n- `fault_type = 'treefall'` events are the direct tree-fall risk signal\n- Document: BBMP tree canopy reference → tree density for the ward\n- Document: BESCOM infra reference → powerline proximity data for the ward\n"
  }
}
```

Prompts (`prompts.py`)\n\nAll instruction strings live here. Never inline strings in agent files. Structure:\n\n```python\nROOT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nCHAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nFLOOD_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nHEAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nINFRA_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nLOGISTICS_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\n```\n\nEach instruction includes:\n- Role and domain scope\n- Explicit list of tools available (agents reason better when told what they can call)\n- Output format for Level-1 domain payload (structured text with labelled sections)\n- Citation requirements\n- Data freshness: include `observed_at` of the most recent record used\n\n### Guardrail strings embedded in CHAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION\n\n```\nOFF_DOMAIN_REFUSAL = \"I can only answer questions about Bengaluru's climate, flood risk, \nair quality, power grid, and traffic. Please rephrase your question.\"\n\nLOCATION_NOT_FOUND = \"I couldn't find '{name}' in the Bengaluru location registry. \nPlease use a ward name or neighbourhood (e.g. 'Bellandur', 'Koramangala', 'Whitefield').\"\n\nNO_DATA_AVAILABLE = \"No recent data is available for {location} on {domain}. \nThe dataset covers historical records — please check the data freshness.\"\n```\n\n---\n\n## 8. Session Persistence Flow\n\n```\nUser → POST /api/chat\n    │\n    ▼\nchat route (FastAPI)\n    │ ─── calls ──→ agent_proxy.get_chat_response(settings, db_session, session_id, message)\n    │                   │\n    │                   ├─ 1. _ensure_session (ADK InMemory)\n    │                   ├─ 2. POST /run → ADK → root_agent → chat_agent → domain agents\n    │                   ├─ 3. extract message_text from events\n    │                   ├─ 4. conversation_service.upsert_session(db_session, session_id)\n    │                   ├─ 5. conversation_service.append_turn(db_session, session_id, ...)\n    │                   └─ 6. await db_session.commit()\n    │\n    ▼\nChatResponse returned to user\n```\n\nThe DB write happens **after** the ADK call succeeds. If the DB write fails, the response is still returned to the user (log the error, don't fail the request). This is a best-effort persistence approach — data availability > perfect persistence.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Environment Variables\n\n### Backend additions (`server/.env`)\n\nNo new variables needed — the backend already has `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN`. The internal routes use it for verification.\n\n"
  }
}
```

**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T09:25:59.019Z

**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T09:25:59.019Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_9kCXvvbeizQ6qClbZDV4UcoM",
    "output": "Chunk ID: afe649\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 2430\nOutput:\n# MetroSense Agent Implementation Spec\n### Full Production Agent — Guardrails, Tools, Sub-agent Delegation, Session Persistence\n### Status: Approved for Implementation\n\n---\n\n## Locked Decisions (from design session)\n\n| Decision | Choice |\n|----------|--------|\n| Data access | Option B — agents call backend internal HTTP API (`X-Internal-Token`) |\n| Documents | Shared volume mount of `server/Documents_Metrosense/` at `DOCUMENTS_PATH` env var |\n| Response mode | Non-streaming — single POST/response, no SSE |\n| ADK session state | `InMemorySessionService` (ADK-side) + write-through to `sessions` + `conversation_history` tables (backend-side, after each turn) |\n| Agent app name | `metrosense_agent` (replaces `metrosearch_agent`) |\n| Root agent name | `root_agent` |\n\n---\n\n## 1. What Changes and Where\n\n### 1.1 Backend changes (`server/`)\n\n| File | Change |\n|------|--------|\n| `app/api/routes/internal.py` | **NEW** — internal data query endpoints, `X-Internal-Token` gated |\n| `app/services/data_service.py` | **NEW** — all SQLAlchemy async queries for internal API |\n| `app/services/conversation_service.py` | **NEW** — session upsert + conversation turn persistence |\n| `app/services/agent_proxy.py` | **UPDATE** — persist session + turn to DB after ADK response |\n| `app/api/router.py` | **UPDATE** — register internal router |\n| `app/core/config.py` | **UPDATE** — `agent_app_name = \"metrosense_agent\"` |\n| `app/db/models.py` | **UPDATE** — add `ConversationHistory`, `AuditLog`, `DBSession` models (they exist in migration but not in models.py) |\n\n### 1.2 Agents changes (`agents/`)\n\n| File | Change |\n|------|--------|\n| `pyproject.toml` | **UPDATE** — add `python-dotenv`, `pydantic>=2.7` |\n| `metrosense_agent/` | **NEW** — full agent package (see tree below) |\n| `metrosearch_agent/` | **KEEP** — do not delete; rename app_name in backend config only |\n| `.env.example` | **UPDATE** — add `BACKEND_INTERNAL_URL`, `DOCUMENTS_PATH` |\n\n### 1.3 Infrastructure changes\n\n| File | Change |\n|------|--------|\n| `docker-compose.yml` | **UPDATE** — agents service: add volume mount + env vars |\n\n---\n\n## 2. New Agents File Tree\n\n```\nagents/\n└── metrosense_agent/\n    ├── __init__.py                   # exports root_agent for ADK\n    ├── agent.py                      # root_agent definition\n    ├── prompts.py                    # all instruction strings (no inline strings in agent files)\n    ├── subagents/\n    │   ├── __init__.py\n    │   ├── chat_agent.py             # chat_agent — sole user-facing agent\n    │   ├── flood_agent.py            # flood_vulnerability_agent\n    │   ├── heat_agent.py             # heat_health_agent\n    │   ├── infra_agent.py            # infrastructure_agent\n    │   └── logistics_agent.py        # logistics_agent\n    └── tools/\n        ├── __init__.py\n        ├── backend_client.py         # shared HTTP client + all backend internal API calls\n        ├── shared/\n        │   ├── __init__.py\n        │   ├── weather_tools.py      # get_weather_current, get_weather_historical\n        │   ├── location_tools.py     # resolve_location, list_locations\n        │   └── document_tools.py     # list_document_indexes, fetch_document_section\n        ├── flood_tools.py            # get_lake_hydrology, get_flood_incidents\n        ├── heat_tools.py             # get_aqi_current, get_aqi_historical, get_ward_profile\n        ├── infra_tools.py            # get_power_outage_events\n        └── logistics_tools.py        # get_traffic_current, get_traffic_corridor\n```\n\n---\n\n## 3. Backend Internal API\n\n### 3.1 Route file: `server/app/api/routes/internal.py`\n\nAll routes prefixed `/internal`. Protected by a FastAPI dependency that reads `X-Internal-Token` from the request header and compares to `settings.agent_internal_token`. Returns HTTP 401 on mismatch. This dependency is **not** the same as `require_user` — no DB lookup, no JWT.\n\n```\nDependency: require_internal_token(request: Request, settings: Settings) -> None\n```\n\n#### Endpoints\n\n| Method | Path | Query params | Returns |\n|--------|------|-------------|---------|\n| GET | `/internal/weather/current` | `location_id: str`, `limit: int = 1` | list of weather observation rows |\n| GET | `/internal/weather/historical` | `location_id: str`, `hours: int = 24` | list of weather observation rows |\n| GET | `/internal/aqi/current` | `location_id: str`, `limit: int = 1` | list of AQI observation rows |\n| GET | `/internal/aqi/historical` | `location_id: str`, `days: int = 7` | list of AQI observation rows |\n| GET | `/internal/lakes` | `lake_id: str`, `limit: int = 5` | list of lake hydrology rows |\n| GET | `/internal/floods` | `location_id: str`, `limit: int = 20` | list of flood incident rows |\n| GET | `/internal/outages` | `location_id: str`, `window_days: int = 30` | list of power outage rows |\n| GET | `/internal/traffic/current` | `location_id: str`, `limit: int = 5` | list of traffic observation rows |\n| GET | `/internal/traffic/corridor` | `corridor_name: str`, `limit: int = 20` | list of traffic observation rows |\n| GET | `/internal/locations/resolve` | `name: str` | list of matching location_master rows |\n| GET | `/internal/ward/profile` | `ward_id: str` | ward_profile row or null |\n\nAll endpoints return JSON arrays (or single object for ward profile). No pagination needed — results are bounded by the `limit` / `window_days` params and the golden dataset size.\n\n#### Response shape convention\n\nEach endpoint returns a plain `list[dict[str, Any]]` — raw column values serialised to JSON. Agents receive this and reason over it. No transformation at the API layer.\n\nTimestamps are serialised as ISO-8601 strings. Booleans remain booleans. Nulls remain nulls.\n\n### 3.2 Service: `server/app/services/data_service.py`\n\nOne async function per endpoint. All use `AsyncSession`. Examples:\n\n```python\nasync def get_weather_current(session, location_id: str, limit: int = 1) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_weather_historical(session, location_id: str, hours: int = 24) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_aqi_current(session, location_id: str, limit: int = 1) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_aqi_historical(session, location_id: str, days: int = 7) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_lake_hydrology(session, lake_id: str, limit: int = 5) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_flood_incidents(session, location_id: str, limit: int = 20) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_power_outages(session, location_id: str, window_days: int = 30) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_traffic_current(session, location_id: str, limit: int = 5) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_traffic_corridor(session, corridor_name: str, limit: int = 20) -> list[dict]\nasync def resolve_location(session, name: str) -> list[dict]\nasync def get_ward_profile(session, ward_id: str) -> dict | None\n```\n\nAll queries use `ORDER BY observed_at DESC` (or equivalent timestamp column) to return most-recent-first. Power outages filter `outage_type != 'planned'` by default (only unplanned outages returned). All use `LIMIT` parameter.\n\n`resolve_location` does a case-insensitive ILIKE search across `canonical_name` and `aliases` (JSON array contains).\n\n### 3.3 Router registration\n\nIn `server/app/api/router.py`, add:\n\n```python\nfrom app.api.routes import internal\nroot_router.include_router(internal.router)\n```\n\nInternal router is **not** wrapped in the `protected_router` (no JWT dependency). It has its own token check dependency.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Conversation Persistence\n\n### 4.1 DB models to add to `server/app/db/models.py`\n\nThree models missing from models.py (they exist in the migration already):\n\n**`DBSession`** (maps to `sessions` table):\n```python\nclass DBSession(Base):\n    __tablename__ = \"sessions\"\n    session_id: Mapped[str]          # PK\n    user_role: Mapped[str]\n    created_at: Mapped[datetime]\n    last_active_at: Mapped[datetime]\n    total_turns: Mapped[int]\n    active_flag: Mapped[bool]\n```\n\n**`ConversationHistory`** (maps to `conversation_history` table):\n```python\nclass ConversationHistory(Base):\n    __tablename__ = \"conversation_history\"\n    turn_id: Mapped[str]             # PK, uuid\n    session_id: Mapped[str]          # FK → sessions.session_id\n    role: Mapped[str]                # \"user\" | \"assistant\"\n    message: Mapped[str]\n    agents_invoked: Mapped[list]     # JSON\n    response_mode: Mapped[str | None]\n    cited_documents: Mapped[list]    # JSON\n    cited_records: Mapped[list]      # JSON\n    timestamp: Mapped[datetime]\n    latency_ms: Mapped[int | None]\n```\n\n**`AuditLog`** (maps to `audit_log` table):\n```python\nclass AuditLog(Base):\n    __tablename__ = \"audit_log\"\n    log_id: Mapped[str]              # PK, uuid\n    session_id: Mapped[str | None]\n    turn_id: Mapped[str | None]\n    query_text: Mapped[str]\n    intent_classified: Mapped[str | None]\n    agents_invoked: Mapped[list]\n    overall_confidence: Mapped[float | None]\n    data_freshness_lag_seconds: Mapped[int | None]\n    stale_sources: Mapped[list]\n    error_flag: Mapped[bool]\n    error_detail: Mapped[str | None]\n    created_at: Mapped[datetime]\n```\n\n### 4.2 Service: `server/app/services/conversation_service.py`\n\n```python\nasync def upsert_session(\n    session: AsyncSession,\n    session_id: str,\n    user_role: str = \"user\",\n) -> None\n```\n- Uses `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (session_id) DO UPDATE SET last_active_at = now(), total_turns = total_turns + 1`\n- If it does not exist: creates with `created_at = now()`, `active_flag = True`, `total_turns = 1`\n\n```python\nasync def append_turn(\n    session: AsyncSession,\n    session_id: str,\n    user_message: str,\n    assistant_message: str,\n"
  }
}
```

All use `LIMIT` parameter.\n\n`resolve_location` does a case-insensitive ILIKE search across `canonical_name` and `aliases` (JSON array contains).\n\n### 3.3 Router registration\n\nIn `server/app/api/router.py`, add:\n\n```python\nfrom app.api.routes import internal\nroot_router.include_router(internal.router)\n```\n\nInternal router is **not** wrapped in the `protected_router` (no JWT dependency). It has its own token check dependency.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Conversation Persistence\n\n### 4.1 DB models to add to `server/app/db/models.py`\n\nThree models missing from models.py (they exist in the migration already):\n\n**`DBSession`** (maps to `sessions` table):\n```python\nclass DBSession(Base):\n    __tablename__ = \"sessions\"\n    session_id: Mapped[str]          # PK\n    user_role: Mapped[str]\n    created_at: Mapped[datetime]\n    last_active_at: Mapped[datetime]\n    total_turns: Mapped[int]\n    active_flag: Mapped[bool]\n```\n\n**`ConversationHistory`** (maps to `conversation_history` table):\n```python\nclass ConversationHistory(Base):\n    __tablename__ = \"conversation_history\"\n    turn_id: Mapped[str]             # PK, uuid\n    session_id: Mapped[str]          # FK → sessions.session_id\n    role: Mapped[str]                # \"user\" | \"assistant\"\n    message: Mapped[str]\n    agents_invoked: Mapped[list]     # JSON\n    response_mode: Mapped[str | None]\n    cited_documents: Mapped[list]    # JSON\n    cited_records: Mapped[list]      # JSON\n    timestamp: Mapped[datetime]\n    latency_ms: Mapped[int | None]\n```\n\n**`AuditLog`** (maps to `audit_log` table):\n```python\nclass AuditLog(Base):\n    __tablename__ = \"audit_log\"\n    log_id: Mapped[str]              # PK, uuid\n    session_id: Mapped[str | None]\n    turn_id: Mapped[str | None]\n    query_text: Mapped[str]\n    intent_classified: Mapped[str | None]\n    agents_invoked: Mapped[list]\n    overall_confidence: Mapped[float | None]\n    data_freshness_lag_seconds: Mapped[int | None]\n    stale_sources: Mapped[list]\n    error_flag: Mapped[bool]\n    error_detail: Mapped[str | None]\n    created_at: Mapped[datetime]\n```\n\n### 4.2 Service: `server/app/services/conversation_service.py`\n\n```python\nasync def upsert_session(\n    session: AsyncSession,\n    session_id: str,\n    user_role: str = \"user\",\n) -> None\n```\n- Uses `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (session_id) DO UPDATE SET last_active_at = now(), total_turns = total_turns + 1`\n- If it does not exist: creates with `created_at = now()`, `active_flag = True`, `total_turns = 1`\n\n```python\nasync def append_turn(\n    session: AsyncSession,\n    session_id: str,\n    user_message: str,\n    assistant_message: str,\n"
  }
}
```

    "call_id": "call_hZVZtlXYS8o1hHme6E0O01vG"

## Entry 55
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T09:26:06.822Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T09:26:06.822Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_snjzyaddg1rbsgmxIorz5nFK",
    "output": "Chunk ID: a75249\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 2127\nOutput:\n\n### 5.1 Flood Vulnerability Agent\n\n**Goal:** Identify flood black spots, predict inundation risk, recommend barricade locations.\n\n**Tools owned directly:**\n```\nPostgres Tools:\n  get_rainfall_current(location_id)\n  get_rainfall_historical(location_id, window_hours)\n  get_lake_hydrology(lake_id)\n  get_flood_incidents_historical(location_id)\n\nDocument RAG Tools:\n  list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)\n  fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)\n```\n\n**Key logic:**\n- Pre-seeded monitored zones: Sarjapur, Bellandur, Manyata Tech Park, Varthur, Hebbal, KR Puram, Koramangala, Silk Board\n- `fill_percentage` + `overflow_status` + `surplus_flow_cusecs` from `lake_hydrology` drives backflow risk\n- Rainfall intensity vs drainage capacity — capacity values extracted from drainage plan document sections\n- 2022 flood post-mortem sections provide calibration priors for black spot scoring\n- Barricade threshold values (mm/hr) come from drainage plan document sections, not hardcoded\n\n**Output — Level-1 FloodRiskPayload (Pydantic):**\n```json\n{\n  \"agent\": \"flood_vulnerability\",\n  \"confidence\": 0.87,\n  \"status\": \"ok\",\n  \"data\": {\n    \"zone_scores\": [],\n    \"blackspots\": [],\n    \"barricade_recommendations\": [],\n    \"lake_status\": {}\n  },\n  \"citations\": [],\n  \"data_freshness\": {}\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### 5.2 Urban Heat & Health Agent\n\n**Goal:** Generate neighborhood-specific health advisories from AQI data and UHI research documents.\n\n**Tools owned directly:**\n```\nPostgres Tools:\n  get_aqi_current(location_id)\n  get_aqi_historical(location_id, days)\n  get_weather_heat_metrics(location_id)\n  get_population_vulnerability(ward_id)\n\nDocument RAG Tools:\n  list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)\n  fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)\n```\n\n**Key logic:**\n- `aqi_category` + `dominant_pollutant` generates specific advisory — not generic messaging\n- `elderly_population_pct` + `children_population_pct` from `ward_profile` flags sensitive population zones\n- `green_cover_pct` from `ward_profile` is the UHI intensity multiplier\n- WHO AQI thresholds applied per pollutant type\n- Temperature + humidity from `weather_observation` drives heat stress tier reasoning\n\n**Output — Level-1 HeatHealthPayload (Pydantic):**\n```json\n{\n  \"agent\": \"heat_health\",\n  \"confidence\": 0.82,\n  \"status\": \"ok\",\n  \"data\": {\n    \"zone_aqi\": [],\n    \"heat_index_map\": {},\n    \"advisories\": [],\n    \"population_risk_tier\": \"HIGH\"\n  },\n  \"citations\": [],\n  \"data_freshness\": {}\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### 5.3 Infrastructure Stress Testing Agent\n\n**Goal:** Predict tree-fall likelihood and power grid failure probability by combining wind telemetry with tree density and historical outage data.\n\n**Tools owned directly:**\n```\nPostgres Tools:\n  get_wind_current(location_id)\n  get_wind_historical(location_id, window_hours)\n  get_power_outage_events(location_id, window_days)\n\nDocument RAG Tools:\n  list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)\n  fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)\n```\n\n**Key logic:**\n- `wind_gust_kmh`, not average wind speed, is the primary tree-fall predictor\n- Tree density and fall-prone species data extracted from BBMP tree density map document sections\n- Powerline proximity data extracted from BESCOM incident report sections\n- `outage_type = planned` is excluded from risk scores — only unplanned outages are relevant\n- `fault_type = treefall` outages in `power_outage_event` are the direct calibration signal\n\n**Output — Level-1 InfraPayload (Pydantic):**\n```json\n{\n  \"agent\": \"infrastructure\",\n  \"confidence\": 0.79,\n  \"status\": \"ok\",\n  \"data\": {\n    \"treefall_risk_by_zone\": {},\n    \"power_outage_probability\": {},\n    \"at_risk_corridors\": [],\n    \"maintenance_alerts\": []\n  },\n  \"citations\": [],\n  \"data_freshness\": {}\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### 5.4 Logistics Disruption Agent\n\n**Goal:** Estimate Time-to-Destination delay factor for delivery fleets by correlating rainfall thresholds with Bangalore traffic bottleneck data.\n\n**Tools owned directly:**\n```\nPostgres Tools:\n  get_traffic_current(location_id)\n  get_traffic_historical(corridor_name, time_band)\n  get_waterlogging_incidents(location_id)\n\nDocument RAG Tools:\n  list_document_indexes(document_type, ward_name)\n  fetch_document_section(document_id, section_id)\n```\n\n**Key logic:**\n- `rainfall_mm_1h` + `corridor_name` + `time_band` + `weekday_weekend_flag` from `traffic_segment_observation` maps to delay factor reasoning\n- `waterlogging_flag` on traffic segments indicates immediate closure risk\n- `heavy_vehicle_share` matters for ORR and Hosur Road — heavier corridors degrade faster in rain\n- Flood post-mortem traffic sections provide historical calibration for corridor-specific thresholds\n- Key monitored corridors: ORR, Sarjapur Road, Hosur Road, Bellary Road, Mysore Road, KR Puram\n\n**Output — Level-1 LogisticsPayload (Pydantic):**\n```json\n{\n  \"agent\": \"logistics\",\n  \"confidence\": 0.84,\n  \"status\": \"ok\",\n  \"data\": {\n    \"ttd_delay_factor_by_zone\": {},\n    \"blocked_routes\": [],\n    \"alt_route_suggestions\": [],\n    \"rainfall_threshold_alerts\": {}\n  },\n  \"citations\": [],\n  \"data_freshness\": {}\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### 5.5 Chat Agent\n\n**Goal:** User-facing conversational layer. Routes to domain agents, synthesizes NL answers, manages session memory, handles follow-up chart prompts.\n\n**Design principle:** Tool-poor, agent-rich. The Chat Agent never queries data directly. Every factual claim it makes is traceable to a domain agent Level-1 payload. This makes hallucination checking deterministic.\n\n**Tools:**\n```\n  session_memory_read()\n  session_memory_write()\n  intent_classifier()\n  call_domain_agent(agent_name, payload)    -- fan-out to domain agents\n  synthesize_nl_response(results[])         -- final LLM call with citations\n  guardrail_check(response)\n  trigger_chart_followup(data, chart_type)  -- appends follow-up prompt if warranted\n```\n\n**Routing examples:**\n```\n\"If 60mm tonight, which underpasses?\"      → Flood + Logistics (parallel)\n\"Compare humidity vs decade average\"       → Heat Agent (historical mode)\n\"Tree-fall risk near MG Road?\"             → Infra Agent (zone filter)\n\"Risk scorecard for Bellandur\"             → All agents → Unified Scorecard\n\"Will ORR delay cross 1.5x tonight?\"       → Logistics Agent only\n```\n\n**[ GAP 3 PLACEHOLDER — Orchestrator Intent Classification Detail ]**\nExact trigger word list, handling for queries spanning 3+ domains simultaneously,\nand merge behavior when one agent returns status: partial — to be defined.\n\n**Chart follow-up behavior:**\nAfter any trend or comparative answer, Chat Agent evaluates whether a chart would improve understanding. If yes it appends \"Would you like to see this as a chart?\" On user confirmation a chart spec is generated and returned. Charts are never generated automatically — always requires explicit user confirmation.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Structured JSON Output Design\n\n### Level-1 — Domain Payload Envelope (every agent returns this shape)\n\n```json\n{\n  \"agent\": \"flood_vulnerability\",\n  \"query_id\": \"uuid-xyz\",\n  \"timestamp\": \"2024-07-15T18:30:00+05:30\",\n  \"confidence\": 0.87,\n  \"status\": \"ok | partial | error\",\n  \"data\": {},\n  \"citations\": [\n    {\n      \"type\": \"database\",\n      \"table\": \"lake_hydrology\",\n      \"record_id\": \"lake_003\",\n      \"field\": \"fill_percentage\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"type\": \"document\",\n      \"document_id\": \"drainage_plan_2019_07\",\n      \"page\": 84,\n      \"section\": \"4.2 ORR Culvert Capacity\"\n    }\n  ],\n  \"data_freshness\": {\n    \"telemetry_lag_seconds\": 42,\n    \"last_pg_query_at\": \"2024-07-15T18:29:58+05:30\",\n    \"stale_sources\": []\n  },\n  \"errors\": []\n}\n```\n\n### Level-2 — Unified Risk Scorecard (Orchestrator pure Python merge)\n\n```json\n{\n  \"scorecard_id\": \"uuid-abc\",\n  \"generated_at\": \"2024-07-15T18:30:05+05:30\",\n  \"query_context\": \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses need barricading?\",\n  \"neighborhoods\": [\n    {\n      \"name\": \"Bellandur\",\n      \"ward_id\": \"ward_042\",\n      \"flood_probability\": 0.91,\n      \"flood_risk_tier\": \"CRITICAL\",\n      \"power_outage_risk\": 0.62,\n      \"traffic_delay_index\": 2.4,\n      \"heat_health_risk\": 0.22,\n      \"treefall_risk\": 0.38,\n      \"barricade_recommended\": true,\n      \"barricade_locations\": [\n        \"Outer Ring Road underpass km 14\",\n        \"Agara underpass\"\n      ],\n      \"emergency_readiness\": {\n        \"recommendation\": \"EVACUATE low-lying streets by 20:00\",\n        \"resource_alert\": \"Pre-position NDRF unit at Bellandur Junction\",\n        \"confidence\": 0.87\n      }\n    }\n  ],\n  \"key_drivers\": [\n"
  }
}
```
