It is the ADK infrastructure that executes the Root Agent, manages the event loop, and handles tool call cycles. You configure it in one line — `Runner(agent=root_agent)`. It is not a component in the architecture diagram.\n \n-**Root Agent:** Pure routing and state management. Never talks to the user directly. Owns parallel dispatch, global guardrails, and the Level-1 to Level-2 scorecard merge.\n+**Root Agent:** Pure routing and state management. Never talks to the user directly. Owns parallel dispatch, global guardrails, and deterministic Level-1 to Level-2 structured block merge.\n \n **Chat Agent:** The only agent that has a conversation with the user. Owns session memory, NL synthesis, citation formatting, chart follow-up prompts, and scorecard triggers. It calls domain agents directly — no round-trip back through the Root Agent.\n \n@@ -197,7 +198,7 @@ The folder structure pre-partitions tool logic by concern inside each agent so s\n │                                                                      │\n │  • Route intent, manage state bag                                    │\n │  • Parallel dispatch via asyncio.gather()                            │\n-│  • Merge Level-1 payloads → Level-2 Scorecard                       │\n+│  • Merge Level-1 payloads → Level-2 structured blocks               │\n │  • Apply global guardrails                                           │\n └───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┘\n                             │\n@@ -225,7 +226,7 @@ AGENT          AGENT         AGENT          AGENT\n                     Each returns Level-1 Pydantic Payload\n                                │\n                     Chat Agent synthesizes NL response\n-                    Root Agent merges → Level-2 Scorecard\n+                    Root Agent merges → Level-2 structured blocks\n ```\n \n ---\n@@ -264,8 +265,8 @@ Flood Agent                      Logistics Agent\n         └──────────────┬───────────────────┘\n                        ▼\n Chat Agent collects Level-1 payloads\n-Root Agent merges → Level-2 Unified Scorecard\n-Chat Agent synthesizes NL answer with citations\n+Root Agent merges → Level-2 structured blocks (risk_card/artifact when applicable)\n+Chat Agent synthesizes canonical Level-2 user response with citations\n         │\n         │ Complete response JSON\n         ▼\n@@ -480,7 +481,7 @@ Document RAG Tools:\n \"If 60mm tonight, which underpasses?\"      → Flood + Logistics (parallel)\n \"Compare humidity vs decade average\"       → Heat Agent (historical mode)\n \"Tree-fall risk near MG Road?\"             → Infra Agent (zone filter)\n-\"Risk scorecard for Bellandur\"             → All agents → Unified Scorecard\n+\"Risk scorecard for Bellandur\"             → All agents → structured `risk_card` block\n \"Will ORR delay cross 1.5x tonight?\"       → Logistics Agent only\n ```\n \n@@ -505,6 +506,15 @@ After any trend or comparative answer, Chat Agent evaluates whether a chart woul\n   \"confidence\": 0.87,\n   \"status\": \"ok | partial | error\",\n   \"data\": {},\n+  \"context_used\": {\n+    \"resolved_location_id\": \"loc_bellandur_001\",\n+    \"resolved_location_name\": \"Bellandur\",\n+    \"intent\": \"flood_risk\",\n+    \"time_window\": \"next_6h\",\n+    \"assumptions\": [\n+      \"Rainfall threshold sourced from drainage_plan_2019_07 section 4.2\"\n+    ]\n+  },\n   \"citations\": [\n     {\n       \"type\": \"database\",\n@@ -528,67 +538,65 @@ After any trend or comparative answer, Chat Agent evaluates whether a chart woul\n }\n ```\n \n-### Level-2 — Unified Risk Scorecard (Orchestrator pure Python merge)\n+`context_used` is mandatory in Level-1 so downstream synthesis remains explainable and auditable.\n+\n+### Level-2 — User Delivery Contract (backend to frontend)\n \n ```json\n {\n-  \"scorecard_id\": \"uuid-abc\",\n-  \"generated_at\": \"2024-07-15T18:30:05+05:30\",\n-  \"query_context\": \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses need barricading?\",\n-  \"neighborhoods\": [\n-    {\n-      \"name\": \"Bellandur\",\n-      \"ward_id\": \"ward_042\",\n-      \"flood_probability\": 0.91,\n-      \"flood_risk_tier\": \"CRITICAL\",\n-      \"power_outage_risk\": 0.62,\n-      \"traffic_delay_index\": 2.4,\n-      \"heat_health_risk\": 0.22,\n-      \"treefall_risk\": 0.38,\n-      \"barricade_recommended\": true,\n-      \"barricade_locations\": [\n-        \"Outer Ring Road underpass km 14\",\n-        \"Agara underpass\"\n-      ],\n-      \"emergency_readiness\": {\n-        \"recommendation\": \"EVACUATE low-lying streets by 20:00\",\n-        \"resource_alert\": \"Pre-position NDRF unit at Bellandur Junction\",\n-        \"confidence\": 0.87\n-      }\n-    }\n-  ],\n-  \"key_drivers\": [\n-    {\n-      \"factor\": \"Rainfall intensity\",\n-      \"value\": \"62mm in 3h\",\n-      \"impact\": \"high\"\n-    },\n+  \"session_id\": \"uuid\",\n+  \"response_mode\": \"text | scorecard | artifact\",\n+  \"response_text\": \"Bellandur flood risk is high for tonight due to rainfall intensity and lake fill levels.\",\n+  \"citations_summary\": [\n     {\n-      \"factor\": \"Drainage capacity gap\",\n-      \"value\": \"Documented undersized culvert near ORR crossing\",\n-      \"impact\": \"high\",\n-      \"citation\": {\n-        \"document_id\": \"drainage_plan_2019_07\",\n-        \"page\": 84\n-      }\n+      \"source_type\": \"database\",\n+      \"source_id\": \"lake_hydrology:lake_003\",\n+      \"label\": \"Bellandur fill percentage\"\n     },\n     {\n-      \"factor\": \"Lake backflow risk\",\n-      \"value\": \"Bellandur fill 92%\",\n-      \"impact\": \"medium\"\n+      \"source_type\": \"document\",\n+      \"source_id\": \"drainage_plan_2019_07#section_4_2\",\n+      \"label\": \"ORR culvert capacity\"\n     }\n   ],\n-  \"global_summary\": {\n-    \"total_zones_analyzed\": 12,\n-    \"critical_zones\": 2,\n-    \"high_risk_zones\": 4,\n-    \"logistics_fleet_advisory\": \"Expect 2.1x average TTD delay on ORR corridor\",\n-    \"overall_emergency_posture\": \"ELEVATED\"\n-  },\n-  \"data_freshness\": {\n+  \"data_freshness_summary\": {\n     \"telemetry_lag_seconds\": 42,\n-    \"last_imd_bulletin\": \"2024-07-15T06:00:00+05:30\",\n-    \"pg_query_time_ms\": 310\n+    \"stale_sources\": []\n+  },\n+  \"risk_card\": null,\n+  \"artifact\": null,\n+  \"follow_up_prompt\": \"Would you like this as a chart?\"\n+}\n+```\n+\n+`risk_card` and `artifact` are nullable and populated only when explicitly requested by the user and supported by implemented capabilities.\n+\n+### Level-2 Structured Block Example — `risk_card`\n+\n+```json\n+{\n+  \"risk_card\": {\n+    \"scorecard_id\": \"uuid-abc\",\n+    \"generated_at\": \"2024-07-15T18:30:05+05:30\",\n+    \"query_context\": \"If it rains 60mm tonight, which underpasses need barricading?\",\n+    \"neighborhoods\": [\n+      {\n+        \"name\": \"Bellandur\",\n+        \"ward_id\": \"ward_042\",\n+        \"flood_probability\": 0.91,\n+        \"flood_risk_tier\": \"CRITICAL\",\n+        \"power_outage_risk\": 0.62,\n+        \"traffic_delay_index\": 2.4,\n+        \"heat_health_risk\": 0.22,\n+        \"treefall_risk\": 0.38,\n+        \"barricade_recommended\": true\n+      }\n+    ],\n+    \"global_summary\": {\n+      \"total_zones_analyzed\": 12,\n+      \"critical_zones\": 2,\n+      \"high_risk_zones\": 4\n+    }\n   }\n }\n ```\n@@ -917,7 +925,7 @@ session_id\n role                   -- user / assistant\n message\n agents_invoked\n-response_mode          -- text / scorecard / chart\n+response_mode          -- text / scorecard / artifact\n cited_documents\n cited_records\n timestamp\n@@ -1357,7 +1365,7 @@ The system is split into three independently deployable layers. Each layer has a\n │  • ADK Runner executes Root Agent                                    │\n │  • Root Agent → Chat Agent → Domain Sub-Agents                      │\n │  • Domain agents connect directly to Postgres + file storage (dev)  │\n-│  • Returns complete structured response — NL text + scorecard JSON   │\n+│  • Returns complete structured response — NL text + nullable blocks  │\n └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n ```\n \n@@ -1375,14 +1383,16 @@ Request:\n   \"user_role\": \"bbmp_internal\"\n }\n \n-Response:\n+Response (canonical Level-2 shape):\n {\n   \"session_id\": \"uuid\",\n+  \"response_mode\": \"text\",\n   \"response_text\": \"Based on current conditions...\",\n-  \"scorecard\": null,\n-  \"chart_followup\": null,\n-  \"citations\": [],\n-  \"data_freshness\": {}\n+  \"citations_summary\": [],\n+  \"data_freshness_summary\": {},\n+  \"risk_card\": null,\n+  \"artifact\": null,\n+  \"follow_up_prompt\": null\n }\n ```\n \n@@ -1397,7 +1407,7 @@ Request:\n   \"user_role\": \"bbmp_internal\"\n }\n \n-Response: same shape as above\n+Response: same canonical Level-2 shape as above\n ```\n \n ---\n@@ -1467,7 +1477,7 @@ urbanclimate_ai/\n │   │   ├── agent.py                     # Root OrchestratorAgent\n │   │   ├── intent_classifier.py         # Maps NL query to domain intents\n │   │   ├── context_manager.py           # Shared session state bag\n-│   │   └── scorecard_merger.py          # Pure Python Level-1 to Level-2 merge\n+│   │   └── scorecard_merger.py          # Pure Python Level-1 to Level-2 structured merge\n │   │\n │   ├── agents/\n │   │   ├── shared/\n"
  }

## Entry 178
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T09:33:37.969Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T09:33:37.969Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_zIzly959WhA1XD3JRSI9CBRj",
    "output": "Chunk ID: b4aa0c\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1571\nOutput:\n```python\nROOT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nCHAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nFLOOD_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nHEAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nINFRA_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\nLOGISTICS_AGENT_INSTRUCTION: str\n```\n\nEach instruction includes:\n- Role and domain scope\n- Explicit list of tools available (agents reason better when told what they can call)\n- Output format for Level-1 domain payload (strict typed JSON envelope)\n- Citation requirements\n- Data freshness: include `observed_at` of the most recent record used\n- Context requirements: include resolved location, time window, and key assumptions in `context_used`\n\n### Guardrail strings embedded in CHAT_AGENT_INSTRUCTION\n\n```\nOFF_DOMAIN_REFUSAL = \"I can only answer questions about Bengaluru's climate, flood risk, \nair quality, power grid, and traffic. Please rephrase your question.\"\n\nLOCATION_NOT_FOUND = \"I couldn't find '{name}' in the Bengaluru location registry. \nPlease use a ward name or neighbourhood (e.g. 'Bellandur', 'Koramangala', 'Whitefield').\"\n\nNO_DATA_AVAILABLE = \"No recent data is available for {location} on {domain}. \nThe dataset covers historical records — please check the data freshness.\"\n```\n\n---\n\n## 8. Session Persistence Flow\n\n```\nUser → POST /api/chat\n    │\n    ▼\nchat route (FastAPI)\n    │ ─── calls ──→ agent_proxy.get_chat_response(settings, db_session, session_id, message)\n    │                   │\n    │                   ├─ 1. _ensure_session (ADK InMemory)\n    │                   ├─ 2. POST /run → ADK → root_agent → chat_agent → domain agents\n    │                   ├─ 3. map ADK output to canonical Level-2 response contract\n    │                   ├─ 4. conversation_service.upsert_session(db_session, session_id)\n    │                   ├─ 5. conversation_service.append_turn(db_session, session_id, ...)\n    │                   └─ 6. await db_session.commit()\n    │\n    ▼\nLevel-2 response returned to user\n```\n\nThe DB write happens **after** the ADK call succeeds. If the DB write fails, the response is still returned to the user (log the error, don't fail the request). This is a best-effort persistence approach — data availability > perfect persistence.\n\n### 8.1 Canonical response contracts (Level-1 vs Level-2)\n\nLevel-1 is internal and mandatory for every domain agent call. Level-2 is the only backend response shape exposed to frontend clients.\n\n**Level-1 domain payload (internal)**\n- Strict typed schema with required fields: `agent`, `query_id`, `timestamp`, `status`, `confidence`, `data`, `citations`, `data_freshness`, `context_used`, `errors`\n- `status` enum is `ok | partial | error`\n- `context_used` is required to capture resolved location, intent, and operational assumptions\n- L1 payloads are not rendered directly in UI\n\n**Level-2 user delivery contract (backend -> frontend)**\n- Single stable shape for all chat responses:\n  - `session_id`\n  - `response_mode` (`text | scorecard | artifact`)\n  - `response_text` (always present)\n  - `citations_summary` (always present, may be empty)\n  - `data_freshness_summary` (always present)\n  - `risk_card` (nullable)\n  - `artifact` (nullable)\n  - `follow_up_prompt` (nullable)\n- `risk_card` and `artifact` are populated only when the user explicitly asks and the capability is implemented; otherwise they remain `null`\n- Mapping from Level-1 -> Level-2 is deterministic (no separate formatting agent)\n\nExample Level-2 response:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"session_id\": \"uuid\",\n  \"response_mode\": \"text\",\n  \"response_text\": \"Bellandur flood risk is elevated tonight due to rainfall and lake fill conditions.\",\n  \"citations_summary\": [],\n  \"data_freshness_summary\": {\n    \"telemetry_lag_seconds\": 42,\n    \"stale_sources\": []\n  },\n  \"risk_card\": null,\n  \"artifact\": null,\n  \"follow_up_prompt\": null\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n## 9. Environment Variables\n\n### Backend additions (`server/.env`)\n\nNo new variables needed — the backend already has `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN`. The internal routes use it for verification.\n\n### Agents additions (`agents/.env`)\n\n```\nBACKEND_INTERNAL_URL=http://localhost:8010    # or http://server:8010 in Docker\nDOCUMENTS_PATH=/documents                     # mounted volume path\nAGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token       # same as backend\nAGENT_MODEL=gemini-2.5-flash\nGOOGLE_API_KEY=<your_key>\n```\n\n### Docker Compose changes (agents service)\n\n```yaml\nagents:\n  ...\n  environment:\n    - BACKEND_INTERNAL_URL=http://server:8010\n    - DOCUMENTS_PATH=/documents\n## 13. Testing Checkpoints\n\n| Checkpoint | What to verify |\n|------------|----------------|\n| Phase 1 done | All 11 internal endpoints return data for known location_ids from the golden dataset |\n| Phase 2 done | `sessions` + `conversation_history` rows written after each `/api/chat` call |\n| Phase 3 done | Each tool function returns non-empty list for a known location_id |\n| Phase 4 done | `adk web` can complete a flood query, a health query, and a scorecard query |\n| Phase 5 done | Full Docker stack end-to-end: frontend chat → backend → proxy → ADK → domain agents → DB |\n\n---\n\n## 14. What Is Out of Scope for This Phase\n\n- Streaming / SSE (confirmed not in scope)\n- AuditLog writes (schema exists, writes added in a later phase after agent invocation tracking is wired)\n- Chart follow-up prompts (agent instruction includes the hook, UI not yet wired)\n- Non-null `risk_card` / `artifact` rendering in the frontend (Level-2 fields exist now but are null until those capabilities are implemented)\n- Sub-agent splitting (current agent files stay flat; sub-agents introduced when any agent > 300 lines)\n- google_search tool removal (metrosearch_agent still exists and works; new agent replaces it for new sessions)\n\n---\n\n## 15. Known Risks\n\n| Risk | Mitigation |\n|------|------------|\n| ADK sub_agent transfer reliability | Test each routing path explicitly in Phase 4 before moving to Phase 5 |\n| `resolve_location` returning zero results | Chat agent instruction handles `LOCATION_NOT_FOUND` case and asks user to rephrase |\n| Documents volume not mounted in dev | `DOCUMENTS_PATH` defaults to a local path; document tools return empty list gracefully on `FileNotFoundError` |\n| DB write fails after ADK response | Best-effort persistence — log error, return response to user regardless |\n"
  }
}
```

    "phase": "final_answer"
  }
}

**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T10:04:38.147Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:04:38.147Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "message",
    "role": "developer",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "input_text",
        "text": "<collaboration_mode># Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n</collaboration_mode>"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions.     "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "none",
    "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository is the working MetroSense codebase: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer (✅ **exists and wired**, current status: skeleton with google_search tool only)\n- Treat the current implementation as the baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n- **Status**: ~40% complete. Auth flow, backend-to-agent routing, and database schema are functional. Data seeding, custom agent tools, risk cards/artifacts, streaming, and message persistence are planned.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Chat UI, streaming presentation, structured response rendering, browser-facing state management.\n- `server/`\n  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\n- `agents/`\n  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\n- `server/app/api/`\n  - FastAPI routes and request/response boundaries.\n- `server/app/services/`\n  - Backend business logic and internal orchestration code.\n- `server/app/db/`\n  - Database models, metadata, and session management.\n- `server/tests/unit/`\n  - Fast isolated tests for backend logic and simple route behavior.\n- `server/tests/integration/`\n  - App-level and database-backed tests.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - The agents service runs behind a lightweight proxy that enforces the `X-Internal-Token` shared secret. The backend calls the proxy on port 8020, and the proxy forwards to the ADK server on port 8021. `/health` stays public for simple availability checks.\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8021 .`\n  - `cd agents && export AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token && uv run uvicorn app.proxy:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n\n### Implemented (✅)\n- **Backend:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout/me with JWT + httponly cookies), health checks, database schema (9+ models for weather/AQI/flooding/outages/traffic), `/api/chat` endpoint\n- **Backend-to-Agent:** Proxy service with `X-Internal-Token` validation, session routing to ADK\n- **Frontend:** Chat UI (MessageBubble, MessageList, InputBar), auth context (login/signup/logout), RiskCard and ArtifactRenderer components, health polling\n- **Tests:** Auth flow (signup/login/logout), password hashing, health checks, basic chat endpoint\n\n### Planned (📋)\n- **Agent Tools:** Custom tools for flood-risk assessment, AQI lookup, outage status, traffic conditions, document retrieval\n- **Data Seeding:** Load CSV data (DataSet_MetroSense/) and documents (Documents_Metrosense/) into database\n- **Structured Responses:** Risk cards and artifacts emitted by agent layer; backend extracts and shapes for frontend\n- **Streaming:** Server-sent events (SSE) or WebSocket for streamed text responses\n- **Message Persistence:** Store chat history in database; load conversation context for agents\n- **Chat Response Tests:** Extend coverage for agent output parsing, risk card generation, artifact rendering\n\n### Current Test Coverage\n- Backend: Health, auth (signup/login/logout/me), password hashing, basic chat routing\n- Frontend: Component renders (no E2E tests written yet)\n- Agents: smoke_test.py validates proxy routing only (not domain logic)\n\n### Next Steps (Recommended Order)\n1. Implement data loader to seed MetroSense CSVs and documents\n2. Add custom agent tools (flood-risk, aqi-lookup, outage-status, traffic)\n3. Implement structured response generation (risk cards/artifacts from agent)\n4. Add message history persistence and context retrieval\n5. Implement streaming responses (SSE or WebSocket)\n6. Extend test coverage for full end-to-end flows\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- **Important:** The CSV data files (DataSet_MetroSense/) and documents (Documents_Metrosense/) exist in the repo but are **not yet loaded** into the database. Implement a data loader before building agent tools that depend on MetroSense domain knowledge.\n- The agent service skeleton runs and routes to google_search tool, but custom domain tools (flood-risk, aqi, outage, traffic) do not yet exist. Add tools incrementally as matching backend data becomes available.\n- If current behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }

}

        "developer_instructions": "# Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response.     "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository is the working MetroSense codebase: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer (✅ **exists and wired**, current status: skeleton with google_search tool only)\n- Treat the current implementation as the baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n- **Status**: ~40% complete. Auth flow, backend-to-agent routing, and database schema are functional. Data seeding, custom agent tools, risk cards/artifacts, streaming, and message persistence are planned.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

shared HTTP client + all backend internal API calls\nPOQ/Agents/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:71:        │   ├── weather_tools.py      # get_weather_current, get_weather_historical\nPOQ/Agents/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:72:        │   ├── location_tools.py     # get_power_outage_events\nPOQ/Agents/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:77:        └── logistics_tools.py        # get_traffic_current, get_traffic_corridor\nPOQ/Agents/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:84:### 3.1 Route file: `server/app/api/routes/internal.py`\nPOQ/Agents/AGENT_IMPLEMENTATION_SPEC.md:86:All routes prefixed `/internal`. ## Entry 209
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T10:04:53.528Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:04:53.528Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_mUXWxtBPFwVNsclDHgKH2uma",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 4743e8\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 772\nOutput:\n\nAdd to `dependencies`:\n```toml\n\"python-dotenv>=1.0\",\n\"pydantic>=2.7\",\n```\n\n`httpx` is already present. `google-adk` is already present.\n\n---\n\n## 12. Implementation Order\n\nExecute in this order. Each step is independently testable before moving to the next.\n\n### Phase 1 — Backend data layer (no agent changes yet)\n\n1. Add `ConversationHistory`, `AuditLog`, `DBSession` models to `server/app/db/models.py`\n2. Implement `server/app/services/data_service.py`\n3. Implement `server/app/api/routes/internal.py` with all 11 endpoints\n4. Register internal router in `server/app/api/router.py`\n5. **Test:** `curl -H \"X-Internal-Token: dev-internal-token\" http://localhost:8010/internal/weather/current?location_id=<id>` returns data\n\n### Phase 2 — Conversation persistence\n\n6. Implement `server/app/services/conversation_service.py`\n7. Update `server/app/services/agent_proxy.py` signature + DB write-through\n8. Update `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` to pass db_session to agent_proxy\n9. Update `server/app/core/config.py` (agent_app_name)\n10. **Test:** After a chat request, rows appear in `sessions` and `conversation_history` tables\n\n### Phase 3 — Agent tools\n\n11. Update `agents/pyproject.toml`\n12. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/backend_client.py`\n13. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/shared/weather_tools.py`\n14. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/shared/location_tools.py`\n15. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/shared/document_tools.py`\n16. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/flood_tools.py`\n17. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/heat_tools.py`\n18. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/infra_tools.py`\n19. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/tools/logistics_tools.py`\n20. **Test:** Import each tool module; call one function against live backend\n\n### Phase 4 — Agents\n\n21. Create `agents/metrosense_agent/prompts.py`\n22. Create domain agent files (flood, heat, infra, logistics)\n23. Create chat_agent\n24. Create root_agent (`agent.py` + `__init__.py`)\n25. **Test:** `uv run adk api_server` picks up `metrosense_agent`; smoke test a flood query\n\n### Phase 5 — Docker + integration\n\n26. Update `docker-compose.yml` (volume + env vars)\n27. Update `agents/.env.example`\n28. Run full smoke test through backend → proxy → ADK → domain agents → DB persistence\n\n---\n\n## 13. Testing Checkpoints\n\n| Checkpoint | What to verify |\n|------------|----------------|\n| Phase 1 done | All 11 internal endpoints return data for known location_ids from the golden dataset |\n| Phase 2 done | `sessions` + `conversation_history` rows written after each `/api/chat` call |\n| Phase 3 done | Each tool function returns non-empty list for a known location_id |\n| Phase 4 done | `adk web` can complete a flood query, a health query, and a scorecard query |\n| Phase 5 done | Full Docker stack end-to-end: frontend chat → backend → proxy → ADK → domain agents → DB |\n\n---\n\n## 14. What Is Out of Scope for This Phase\n\n- Streaming / SSE (confirmed not in scope)\n- AuditLog writes (schema exists, writes added in a later phase after agent invocation tracking is wired)\n"
  }
}
```

    "output": "Chunk ID: 377b2e\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1146\nOutput:\n{\n  \"document_id\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022\",\n  \"title\": \"Urban Flood Vulnerability and Inundation Dynamics in Bangalore — A Technical Study\",\n  \"agency\": \"Indian Institute of Science (IISc) — Centre for Infrastructure, Sustainable Transportation and Urban Planning (CiSTUP)\",\n  \"document_type\": \"flood_research\",\n  \"published_at\": \"2022-11\",\n  \"wards_covered\": [\n    \"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\n    \"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Hebbal\",\"Whitefield\",\"HSR Layout\"\n  ],\n  \"source_path\": \"/documents/raw/bangalore_flood_study_2022.pdf\",\n  \"total_pages\": 112,\n  \"total_sections\": 5,\n  \"summary\": \"Technical research study examining urban flood vulnerability in Bangalore with empirical analysis of flood events from 2015–2022. Establishes rainfall-inundation correlation curves for major wards, analyses lake backflow contribution to road inundation, provides corridor-level delay multipliers for logistics planning, and recommends ward-level early warning thresholds. Calibration reference for the UrbanClimate AI flood and logistics agents.\",\n  \"table_of_contents\": [\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s1\",\n      \"title\": \"Study Scope, Methodology, and Ward Coverage\",\n      \"page_start\": 1, \"page_end\": 18,\n      \"summary\": \"Overview of study design, data sources used, spatial coverage, and field survey methodology. Covers 10 high-risk wards with documented flood history from 2015–2022. Establishes the analytical framework used in subsequent sections.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Hebbal\",\"Whitefield\",\"HSR Layout\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s2\",\n      \"title\": \"Rainfall-Inundation Correlation Findings by Ward\",\n      \"page_start\": 19, \"page_end\": 48,\n      \"summary\": \"Core empirical findings — rainfall intensity and duration thresholds that produce measurable road inundation in each study ward. Includes antecedent soil moisture correction factors and prior-day rainfall cumulative effects on inundation probability.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s2.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s3\",\n      \"title\": \"Lake Backflow Contribution Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 49, \"page_end\": 72,\n      \"summary\": \"Quantitative analysis of how lake fill levels amplify surface flood risk. Isolates lake backflow contribution from direct rainfall contribution for Bellandur, Varthur, and KR Puram lake systems. Provides compound probability curves for joint lake-fill and rainfall events.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s3.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s4\",\n      \"title\": \"Road Corridor Vulnerability Rankings and Delay Multipliers\",\n      \"page_start\": 73, \"page_end\": 94,\n      \"summary\": \"Empirical delay multipliers for six major Bangalore road corridors as a function of rainfall intensity band. Based on GPS probe vehicle data and incident records. Provides logistics planning reference for estimating travel time degradation under rain conditions.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Silk Board\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s5\",\n      \"title\": \"Early Warning Thresholds and Recommendations\",\n      \"page_start\": 95, \"page_end\": 112,\n      \"summary\": \"Research-derived early warning threshold recommendations for each study ward. Covers lead time requirements, recommended monitoring parameters, and threshold values calibrated against observed flood events. Intended for integration into BBMP disaster management protocols.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s5.md\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n"

Primary reference for flood vulnerability assessment and underpass risk scoring.\",\n  \"table_of_contents\": [\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s1\",\n      \"title\": \"Overview and Scope of Bangalore Drainage Network\",\n      \"page_start\": 1, \"page_end\": 18,\n      \"summary\": \"Background on Bangalore stormwater drainage network — total channel length, primary and secondary drain classifications, lake interconnection structure, and coverage gaps.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s1.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s2\",\n      \"title\": \"ORR Corridor Culvert and Underpass Capacity Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 42, \"page_end\": 67,\n      \"summary\": \"Hydraulic capacity analysis of all major underpasses and culverts on the Outer Ring Road corridor. Includes design discharge values, observed failure thresholds, and barricade deployment trigger points.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s2.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s3\",\n      \"title\": \"Sarjapur Road and Varthur Catchment Drainage Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 68, \"page_end\": 94,\n      \"summary\": \"Catchment analysis for Sarjapur Road corridor and Varthur lake sub-basin. Documents drainage channel sizing, culvert conditions, lake outfall capacities, and road inundation risk. Varthur lake overflow behaviour and downstream impact on Sarjapur Road analysed in detail.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"HSR Layout\",\"Koramangala\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s4\",\n      \"title\": \"North Bangalore Drainage — Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Corridor\",\n      \"page_start\": 95, \"page_end\": 118,\n      \"summary\": \"Drainage infrastructure assessment for north Bangalore including Hebbal lake system, Yelahanka tank cascade, and Manyata Tech Park catchment. Covers Bellary Road underpasses and Hebbal flyover drainage choke point.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Manyata Tech Park\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s5\",\n      \"title\": \"Critical Failure Zones, Threshold Summary, and Barricade Trigger Matrix\",\n      \"page_start\": 152, \"page_end\": 174,\n      \"summary\": \"Consolidated reference table of all known drainage failure zones across BBMP limits. Specifies exact rainfall intensity thresholds (mm/hr) and lake fill percentages at which each underpass and road segment is recommended for barricading. Primary lookup table for BBMP disaster management teams.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"Hebbal\",\"KR Puram\",\"Whitefield\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s5.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s6\",\n      \"title\": \"Ward-Level Drainage Capacity Ratings and Infrastructure Gaps\",\n      \"page_start\": 175, \"page_end\": 198,\n      \"summary\": \"Summary ratings for all 16 BBMP wards. Identifies wards requiring priority investment.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Manyata Tech Park\",\"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\"Yeshwanthpur\",\"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s6.md\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n"

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:05:17.342Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_0G5eo6O1xMP6NcYoGxGuF3gH",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 602c96\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1231\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom typing import Annotated, Any\n\nfrom fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, Header, HTTPException, Query, status\nfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession\n\nfrom app.api.deps import db_session, settings\nfrom app.core.config import Settings\nfrom app.services import data_service\n\nrouter = APIRouter(prefix=\"/internal\", tags=[\"internal\"])\n\n\ndef require_internal_token(\n    x_internal_token: Annotated[str | None, Header(alias=\"X-Internal-Token\")] = None,\n    app_settings: Settings = Depends(settings),\n) -> None:\n    expected = app_settings.agent_internal_token.strip()\n    if not expected or x_internal_token != expected:\n        raise HTTPException(\n            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,\n            detail=\"Invalid internal token\",\n        )\n\n\n@router.get(\n    \"/weather/current\",\n    dependencies=[Depends(require_internal_token)],\n)\nasync def weather_current(\n    location_id: str,\n    limit: int = Query(default=1, ge=1, le=500),\n    session: AsyncSession = Depends(db_session),\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    return await data_service.get_weather_current(session, location_id=location_id, limit=limit)\n\n\n@router.get(\n    \"/weather/historical\",\n    dependencies=[Depends(require_internal_token)],\n)\nasync def weather_historical(\n    location_id: str,\n    hours: int = Query(default=24, ge=1, le=720),\n    session: AsyncSession = Depends(db_session),\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    return await data_service.get_weather_historical(session, location_id=location_id, hours=hours)\n\n\n@router.get(\n    \"/aqi/current\",\n    dependencies=[Depends(require_internal_token)],\n)\nasync def aqi_current(\n    location_id: str,\n    limit: int = Query(default=1, ge=1, le=500),\n    session: AsyncSession = Depends(db_session),\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    return await data_service.get_aqi_current(session, location_id=location_id, limit=limit)\n\n\n@router.get(\n    \"/aqi/historical\",\n    dependencies=[Depends(require_internal_token)],\n)\nasync def aqi_historical(\n    location_id: str,\n    days: int = Query(default=7, ge=1, le=365),\n    session: AsyncSession = Depends(db_session),\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    return await data_service.get_aqi_historical(session, location_id=location_id, days=days)\n\n\n@router.get(\n    \"/lakes\",\n    dependencies=[Depends(require_internal_token)],\n)\nasync def lakes(\n    lake_id: str,\n    limit: int = Query(default=5, ge=1, le=500),\n    session: AsyncSession = Depends(db_session),\n) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    return await data_service.get_lake_hydrology(session, lake_id=lake_id, limit=limit)\n\n\n@router.get(\n    \"/floods\",\n    dependencies=[Depends(require_internal_token)],\n)\nasync def floods(\n    location_id: str,\n    limit: int = Query(default=20, ge=1, le=500),\n    session: AsyncSession = Depends(db_session),\n) -> PowerOutageEvent.started_at >= window_start,\n            or_(\n                PowerOutageEvent.outage_type.is_(None),\n                func.lower(PowerOutageEvent.outage_type) != \"planned\",\n            ),\n        )\n        .order_by(PowerOutageEvent.started_at.desc())\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync .order_by(TrafficSegmentObservation.observed_at.desc())\n        .limit(limit)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def resolve_location(session: AsyncSession, name: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    pattern = f\"%{name}%\"\n    stmt = (\n        select(LocationMaster)\n        .where(\n            or_(\n                LocationMaster.canonical_name.ilike(pattern),\n                cast(LocationMaster.aliases, String).ilike(pattern),\n            )\n        )\n        .order_by(LocationMaster.canonical_name.asc())\n        .limit(20)\n    )\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def list_locations(session: AsyncSession, limit: int = 200) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:\n    stmt = select(LocationMaster).order_by(LocationMaster.canonical_name.asc()).limit(limit)\n    rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()\n    return [_row_to_dict(row) for row in rows]\n\n\nasync def get_ward_profile(session: AsyncSession, ward_id: str) -> dict[str, Any] | None:\n    stmt = select(WardProfile).where(WardProfile.ward_id == ward_id).limit(1)\n    row = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().first()\n    if row is None:\n        return None\n    return _row_to_dict(row)\n"

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:05:24.958Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_XdGEdXC2Oi1NWpZDPItRJdPt",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 137036\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 261\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport os\nimport sys\n\nimport httpx\n\n\ndef main() -> int:\n    base_url = os.getenv(\"SERVER_URL\", \"http://localhost:8010\")\n    timeout = httpx.Timeout(30.0)\n\n    with httpx.Client(timeout=timeout) as client:\n        health = client.get(f\"{base_url}/api/health\")\n        if health.status_code != 200:\n            print(\"health check failed:\", health.status_code, health.text)\n            return 1\n\n        chat = client.post(\n            f\"{base_url}/api/chat\",\n            json={\"session_id\": \"smoke-test\", \"message\": \"What is Bengaluru's flood risk today?\"},\n        )\n        if chat.status_code != 200:\n            print(\"chat request failed:\", chat.status_code, chat.text)\n            return 1\n\n        payload = chat.json()\n        if \"message\" not in payload:\n            print(\"chat response missing message:\", payload)\n            return 1\n\n        print(\"health ok:\", health.json())\n        print(\"chat ok:\", payload[\"message\"])\n\n    return 0\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    raise SystemExit(main())\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 245
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T10:05:24.964Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:05:24.964Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_NwGfUNbhOBY4kpHVXMRCd6UO",
    "output": "Chunk ID: a5852f\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 673\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport os\nfrom typing import Iterable\n\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\nimport httpx\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI, Header, HTTPException, Request, Response\n\n# Load environment variables from .env file\nload_dotenv()\n\napp = FastAPI(title=\"MetroSense Agents Proxy\")\n\nADK_BASE_URL = os.getenv(\"ADK_BASE_URL\", \"http://127.0.0.1:8021\").rstrip(\"/\")\nINTERNAL_TOKEN = os.getenv(\"AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN\", \"\").strip()\n\n\ndef _require_internal_token(header_value: str | None) -> None:\n    if not INTERNAL_TOKEN:\n        raise RuntimeError(\"AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN must be set for the agents proxy\")\n    if not header_value or header_value != INTERNAL_TOKEN:\n        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail=\"Invalid internal token\")\n\n\ndef _filtered_request_headers(headers: Iterable[tuple[str, str]]) -> dict[str, str]:\n    blocked = {\"host\", \"content-length\", \"connection\", \"x-internal-token\"}\n    return {key: value for key, value in headers if key.lower() not in blocked}\n\n\ndef _filtered_response_headers(headers: Iterable[tuple[str, str]]) -> dict[str, str]:\n    blocked = {\n        \"content-length\",\n        \"connection\",\n        \"transfer-encoding\",\n        \"content-encoding\",\n    }\n    return {key: value for key, value in headers if key.lower() not in blocked}\n\n\n@app.get(\"/health\")\nasync def health() -> Response:\n    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:\n        response = await client.get(f\"{ADK_BASE_URL}/health\")\n    response_headers = _filtered_response_headers(response.headers.items())\n    return Response(\n        content=response.content,\n        status_code=response.status_code,\n        headers=response_headers,\n        media_type=response.headers.get(\"content-type\"),\n    )\n\n\n@app.api_route(\"/{path:path}\", methods=[\"GET\", \"POST\", \"PUT\", \"PATCH\", \"DELETE\", \"OPTIONS\"])\nasync def proxy(\n    request: Request,\n    path: str,\n    x_internal_token: str | None = Header(default=None, alias=\"X-Internal-Token\"),\n) -> Response:\n    _require_internal_token(x_internal_token)\n    target_url = f\"{ADK_BASE_URL}/{path}\"\n    body = await request.body()\n    headers = _filtered_request_headers(request.headers.items())\n\n    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:\n        response = await client.request(\n            request.method,\n            target_url,\n            params=request.query_params,\n            content=body,\n            headers=headers,\n        )\n\n    response_headers = _filtered_response_headers(response.headers.items())\n    return Response(\n        content=response.content,\n        status_code=response.status_code,\n        headers=response_headers,\n        media_type=response.headers.get(\"content-type\"),\n    )\n"
  }
}
```

httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:\n        response = await client.get(f\"{ADK_BASE_URL}/health\")\n    response_headers = _filtered_response_headers(response.headers.items())\n    return Response(\n        content=response.content,\n        status_code=response.status_code,\n        headers=response_headers,\n        media_type=response.headers.get(\"content-type\"),\n    )\n\n\n@app.api_route(\"/{path:path}\", methods=[\"GET\", \"POST\", \"PUT\", \"PATCH\", \"DELETE\", \"OPTIONS\"])\nasync def proxy(\n    request: Request,\n    path: str,\n    x_internal_token: str | None = Header(default=None, alias=\"X-Internal-Token\"),\n) -> Response:\n    _require_internal_token(x_internal_token)\n    target_url = f\"{ADK_BASE_URL}/{path}\"\n    body = await request.body()\n    headers = _filtered_request_headers(request.headers.items())\n\n    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:\n        response = await client.request(\n            request.method,\n            target_url,\n            params=request.query_params,\n            content=body,\n            headers=headers,\n        )\n\n    response_headers = _filtered_response_headers(response.headers.items())\n    return Response(\n        content=response.content,\n        status_code=response.status_code,\n        headers=response_headers,\n        media_type=response.headers.get(\"content-type\"),\n    )\n"
  }
}
```

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:05:40.468Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call",
    "name": "request_user_input",
    "arguments": "{\"questions\":[{\"header\":\"Doc path mode\",\"id\":\"doc_path_mode\",\"question\":\"How should document tools resolve document files across local dev and Docker volume mounts?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Fallback path chain (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Use `DOCUMENTS_PATH` first, then auto-fallback to common local path (`server/Documents_Metrosense`) if unset/missing.\"},{\"label\":\"Strict env only\",\"description\":\"Require `DOCUMENTS_PATH` to be valid; return errors/empty if not configured.\"},{\"label\":\"Dual env vars\",\"description\":\"Use separate env vars for local and docker and choose by runtime flag.\"}]},{\"header\":\"Tool errors\",\"id\":\"tool_error_behavior\",\"question\":\"When backend HTTP calls fail or document files are unreadable, what should tools return?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Graceful empty + metadata (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Return empty results plus lightweight error metadata field so agents can reason about degraded confidence.\"},{\"label\":\"Graceful empty only\",\"description\":\"Return only []/null on failure with logs; no explicit metadata in tool output.\"},{\"label\":\"Raise exceptions\",\"description\":\"Fail fast and let ADK handle tool errors directly.\"}]},{\"header\":\"Read filters\",\"id\":\"doc_filtering\",\"question\":\"For `list_document_indexes`, should we add server-side filtering arguments now?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Include filters now (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Support optional `document_type`, `ward_name`, `contains_thresholds` filtering to reduce prompt noise.\"},{\"label\":\"No filters now\",\"description\":\"Return all index docs and let the LLM filter in prompt reasoning.\"},{\"label\":\"Only doc_type filter\",\"description\":\"Keep minimal filter surface, add ward/flags later.\"}]}]}",
    "call_id": "call_A9w7xMaacpG2wV5MbrBjhqHG"
  }
}
```

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T10:06:54.860Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call",
    "name": "request_user_input",
    "arguments": "{\"questions\":[{\"header\":\"Meta shape\",\"id\":\"tool_meta_shape\",\"question\":\"How do you want error/degraded metadata exposed from tools?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Common envelope (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Every tool returns `{data, meta}` consistently; `meta` carries `ok`, `error_code`, `source`.\"},{\"label\":\"Data-only + logs\",\"description\":\"Keep existing list/dict return types and record metadata only in logs.\"},{\"label\":\"Inline sentinel rows\",\"description\":\"Keep list return type and include a synthetic metadata row when failures occur.\"}]}]}",
    "call_id": "call_nbixBHZ64IA22KCgpNbZGFsN"
  }
}
```
